name: AP Statistics
description: AP Statistics exam preparation following the College Board Course and Exam Description. Students study exploring data, sampling and experimentation, probability and simulation, and statistical inference while preparing for the College Board AP Statistics exam.

modules.title: Exploring Data: Categorical & Quantitative Variables
modules.titleZh: 探索数据：分类变量与定量变量
modules.objectives: Distinguish categorical from quantitative variables; construct and interpret frequency tables, bar charts, pie charts, and dotplots; describe distribution shape, center, and spread.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1: Sampling and Data
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Classify 20 variables as categorical or quantitative. Create a frequency table and bar chart for a provided dataset. Write a 3-sentence description of the distribution. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Exploring Data: Categorical & Quantitative Variables module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Describing Distributions
modules.objectives: Compute and interpret mean, median, mode, range, IQR, variance, and standard deviation; construct and interpret histograms, stemplots, and boxplots; identify outliers using the 1.5*IQR rule.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2: Descriptive Statistics
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: For a given dataset: compute mean, median, Q1, Q3, IQR, and standard deviation. Construct a boxplot and histogram. Identify any outliers using the 1.5*IQR rule. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Describing Distributions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Comparing Distributions
modules.objectives: Compare distributions using parallel boxplots and back-to-back stemplots; describe differences in shape, center, spread, and unusual features in context; use z-scores to compare values across distributions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2.7-2.8: Comparing Distributions
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Compare two datasets using parallel boxplots. Calculate z-scores for 6 values across two different distributions. Write a 2-paragraph comparison in context. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Comparing Distributions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Bivariate Data & Linear Regression
modules.objectives: Create and interpret scatterplots; describe association (direction, form, strength); calculate and interpret the correlation coefficient r and coefficient of determination r^2; find and use the least-squares regression line.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12: Linear Regression and Correlation
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Correlation and Linear Regression
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: For a bivariate dataset: create a scatterplot, calculate r, find the LSRL, interpret slope and intercept in context, and compute r^2. Identify any influential points. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Bivariate Data & Linear Regression module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Designing Studies
modules.objectives: Distinguish observational studies from experiments; identify sampling methods (SRS, stratified, cluster, systematic); recognize sources of bias; understand principles of experimental design (randomization, control, replication, blinding).
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.2-1.3: Sampling and Study Design
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Design a study to investigate a question of your choice. Specify the sampling method, control group, treatment, and how to address potential confounding variables. Write a 1-page study proposal. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Designing Studies module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Probability
modules.objectives: Apply the addition rule, multiplication rule, and complement rule; compute conditional probabilities; use Venn diagrams and two-way tables; distinguish independent from dependent events. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 3: Probability Topics
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Solve 10 probability problems using addition, multiplication, and complement rules. Construct a two-way table and use it to calculate 4 conditional probabilities. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Probability module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Random Variables
modules.objectives: Define discrete and continuous random variables; compute expected value (mean) and standard deviation of a random variable; apply rules for transforming and combining random variables.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 4: Discrete Random Variables
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Construct a probability distribution table for a given scenario. Calculate the mean and standard deviation. Apply linear transformation and combination rules for 4 problems. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Random Variables module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Binomial & Geometric Distributions
modules.objectives: Verify BINS conditions for a binomial setting; calculate binomial probabilities using the formula and technology; compute mean and standard deviation of a binomial; understand the geometric distribution. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 4.3: Binomial Distribution
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Verify BINS for 4 scenarios. Calculate P(X=k), P(X<=k), and P(X>k) for 3 binomial settings. Solve 2 geometric distribution problems. Submit your work as a labeled solution set that includes: (1) each major calculation or transformation step, (2) a short explanation of why the method fits the problem, (3) one checked or corrected answer with the error explained, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Binomial & Geometric Distributions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Sampling Distributions
modules.objectives: Define and describe the sampling distribution of the sample mean and sample proportion; apply the Central Limit Theorem; calculate and interpret standard error.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 7: The Central Limit Theorem
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — The Central Limit Theorem
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Calculate the mean and standard error of sampling distributions for 4 scenarios. Use the CLT to find probabilities for sample means. Explain the effect of sample size on variability. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Sampling Distributions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Confidence Intervals
modules.objectives: Construct and interpret confidence intervals for a population mean and proportion; understand the meaning of confidence level; analyze the effect of sample size and confidence level on interval width.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 8: Confidence Intervals
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Construct 95% and 99% confidence intervals for 3 datasets (means and proportions). Interpret each interval in context. Determine the required sample size for a desired margin of error. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Confidence Intervals module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Significance Tests
modules.objectives: State hypotheses (H0 and Ha); calculate and interpret a test statistic and p-value; make and justify a conclusion at a given significance level alpha; understand Type I and Type II errors and power.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 9: Hypothesis Testing with One Sample
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Conduct full hypothesis tests (4-step process) for 4 scenarios involving means and proportions. Identify and describe Type I and Type II errors in each context. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Significance Tests module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Comparing Two Populations
modules.objectives: Construct confidence intervals and conduct significance tests for two-sample means (independent and paired) and two-sample proportions; select the appropriate procedure and verify conditions. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 10: Hypothesis Testing with Two Samples
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Two-Sample t-Tests and Confidence Intervals
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Conduct two-sample hypothesis tests for 3 scenarios (means and proportions). Construct and interpret 95% confidence intervals for each. Write conclusions in full sentences in context. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Comparing Two Populations module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Chi-Square Tests
modules.objectives: Perform chi-square goodness-of-fit tests; chi-square tests of independence; chi-square tests of homogeneity; compute test statistics, degrees of freedom, and p-values; interpret results in context. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 11: The Chi-Square Distribution
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

Write a full conclusion. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Chi-Square Tests module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Inference for Linear Regression
modules.objectives: Verify conditions for regression inference; construct and interpret a confidence interval for the slope; test H0: beta=0 using a t-test for slope; interpret computer regression output.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12: Linear Regression and Correlation Inference
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

modules.assignment: Interpret a regression computer output table. Test the hypothesis that the slope is zero at alpha=0.05. Construct a 95% CI for the slope and interpret it in context. Verify all conditions. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Inference for Linear Regression module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Inference for Linear Regression — Advanced Applications

modules.objectives: Analyze linear regression inference problems by checking conditions, interpreting slope and interval estimates, evaluating residual evidence, and communicating conclusions in context. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.

Create a scatterplot and residual plot, check inference conditions, compute or interpret a confidence interval for slope, and write one AP-style conclusion in context. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Inference for Linear Regression — Advanced Applications module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12: Linear Regression and Correlation Inference (Advanced)

Score the set with a rubric and revise the lowest-scoring response. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Inference for Linear Regression — Synthesis & Review module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a categorical variable?
quizQuestions.explanation: Categorical variables place individuals into groups or categories. Favorite color is categorical; height, SAT score, and number of siblings are quantitative.

quizQuestions.question: A distribution is described as skewed right. Where is the tail?
quizQuestions.explanation: A right-skewed distribution has a long tail extending toward higher (right) values.

quizQuestions.question: A pie chart best displays which type of data?
options: Categorical data as parts of a whole
quizQuestions.answer: Categorical data as parts of a whole
quizQuestions.explanation: Pie charts display how categorical data is divided into parts of a whole, showing relative frequencies.

quizQuestions.question: Which variable is categorical rather than quantitative?
quizQuestions.explanation: Favorite color places individuals into named groups and has no numerical measurement scale.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which graph is best for displaying a single quantitative variable for a small dataset?
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Dotplot" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Which measure of center is most resistant to outliers?
quizQuestions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers because it depends only on the middle value(s), not extreme values.

quizQuestions.question: The interquartile range (IQR) is calculated as:
quizQuestions.explanation: IQR = Q3 - Q1, representing the range of the middle 50% of data. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Describing Distributions.

quizQuestions.question: By the 1.5*IQR rule, a value is an outlier if it is:
options: More than 2 SD from the mean
More than 3 SD from the mean
Less than Q1 or greater than Q3
quizQuestions.explanation: A value is an outlier if it falls below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR.

quizQuestions.question: For a right-skewed distribution, which is typically true?
options: Mean and Median are both zero
quizQuestions.explanation: In a right-skewed distribution, the mean is pulled toward the high outliers, making mean > median.

quizQuestions.question: Which measure of center is most resistant to extreme outliers?
quizQuestions.explanation: The median depends on the middle position, so a few extreme values do not move it as much as they move the mean.

quizQuestions.question: A z-score of -2 means a value is:
quizQuestions.explanation: z = (x - mean)/sd. A z-score of -2 means the value is 2 standard deviations below the mean.

quizQuestions.question: Which graph allows direct comparison of five-number summaries for two distributions?
quizQuestions.explanation: Parallel boxplots display the five-number summary side by side for easy comparison.

quizQuestions.question: A student scores 80 on Test A (mean=70, sd=10) and 75 on Test B (mean=60, sd=5). On which did she perform relatively better?
quizQuestions.explanation: z_A = (80-70)/10 = 1.0. z_B = (75-60)/5 = 3.0. Higher z-score means better relative performance, so Test B.

quizQuestions.question: What does a z-score of -2 indicate?
options: The value is 2 standard deviations below the mean
The value is 2 units below zero
The value is twice the mean
The value is 2 standard deviations above the mean
quizQuestions.answer: The value is 2 standard deviations below the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: A negative z-score is below the mean, and its magnitude gives the number of standard deviations from the mean.

quizQuestions.question: Which display is best for directly comparing five-number summaries of two distributions?
quizQuestions.explanation: Parallel boxplots show medians, quartiles, ranges, and possible outliers side by side for comparison.

quizQuestions.question: A scatterplot shows a negative association. This means:
options: As x increases, y tends to increase
As x increases, y tends to decrease
There is no relationship between x and y
x and y are always equal
quizQuestions.answer: As x increases, y tends to decrease
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative association means an inverse relationship: higher x values tend to be paired with lower y values.

quizQuestions.question: r = 0.92 indicates:
quizQuestions.explanation: r close to +1 indicates a strong positive linear association. r = 0.92 is very close to 1.

quizQuestions.question: In Bivariate Data & Linear Regression, what does r^2 = 0.81 mean?
options: 81% of the variation in y is explained by the linear model
19% of data points fit the line
quizQuestions.answer: 81% of the variation in y is explained by the linear model
quizQuestions.explanation: r^2 represents the proportion of variability in y that is explained by the linear regression on x.

quizQuestions.question: In the LSRL y-hat = 2.3 + 0.5x, what does 0.5 represent?
options: For each 1-unit increase in x, y increases by 0.5 on average
quizQuestions.answer: For each 1-unit increase in x, y increases by 0.5 on average
quizQuestions.explanation: The slope of the LSRL represents the predicted change in y for each 1-unit increase in x.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Correlation does not imply.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Causation" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: A simple random sample (SRS) ensures:
options: Every individual has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.answer: Every individual has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.explanation: In an SRS, every individual in the population has an equal probability of being chosen.

quizQuestions.question: In Designing Studies, Confounding occurs when:
options: The sample size is too small
An extraneous variable is associated with both the explanatory variable and the response
The experiment has no control group
quizQuestions.answer: An extraneous variable is associated with both the explanatory variable and the response
quizQuestions.explanation: Confounding means an outside variable is mixed up with the explanatory variable, making it impossible to isolate the true effect.

quizQuestions.question: Which principle prevents systematic differences between experimental groups?
quizQuestions.explanation: Randomization (randomly assigning subjects to treatments) prevents systematic bias between treatment groups.

quizQuestions.question: Stratified sampling vs. cluster sampling: the key difference is:
options: Stratified samples from each group; cluster selects entire groups
Stratified divides into groups and samples all; cluster divides and samples some
Cluster is random; stratified is not
quizQuestions.answer: Stratified samples from each group; cluster selects entire groups
quizQuestions.explanation: Stratified: divide into homogeneous strata, then sample from each. Cluster: divide into heterogeneous clusters, then randomly select entire clusters.

quizQuestions.question: Nonresponse bias occurs when:
quizQuestions.explanation: Nonresponse bias results when individuals who do not respond are systematically different from those who do.

quizQuestions.question: What is a defining feature of a simple random sample?
options: Every individual receives the same treatment
Every individual has an equal chance of selection
The population is divided into clusters first
quizQuestions.answer: Every individual has an equal chance of selection
quizQuestions.explanation: In the basic description of an SRS, individuals are selected by chance so each individual has an equal chance to be chosen.

quizQuestions.question: Two events A and B are independent if:
quizQuestions.explanation: A and B are independent if knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A.

quizQuestions.question: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The general addition rule avoids double-counting the overlap between A and B.

quizQuestions.question: If P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.5 and A and B are mutually exclusive, what is P(A or B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Mutually exclusive means P(A and B) = 0. So P(A or B) = 0.4 + 0.5 - 0 = 0.9.

quizQuestions.question: Which condition shows that events A and B are independent?
quizQuestions.explanation: If knowing that B occurred does not change the probability of A, then A and B are independent.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: ___ is the probability of rolling a sum of 7 on two fair dice.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 6/36.

quizQuestions.question: The expected value E(X) of a discrete random variable is:
options: The sum of all possible values
The sum of x_i * P(x_i) for all values
quizQuestions.answer: The sum of x_i * P(x_i) for all values
quizQuestions.explanation: E(X) = sum of [x_i * P(x_i)], the weighted average of all possible values. This reinforces the Random Variables idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: If E(X) = 5 and Y = 3X + 2, what is E(Y)?
quizQuestions.explanation: E(aX + b) = a*E(X) + b = 3(5) + 2 = 17. This reinforces the Random Variables idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: If X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 4 and Var(Y) = 9, what is Var(X + Y)?
quizQuestions.explanation: For independent variables, Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) = 4 + 9 = 13. It connects the correct answer to Random Variables and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: How is the expected value of a discrete random variable calculated?
options: Add all probabilities without using outcomes
Multiply each value by its probability and sum the products
Average the smallest and largest possible values
Choose the value with the highest probability
quizQuestions.answer: Multiply each value by its probability and sum the products
quizQuestions.explanation: For a discrete random variable, E(X) is the sum of x_i times P(x_i) across all possible values.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Sd(ax) =.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "|a| * SD(X)" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: What are the BINS conditions for a binomial distribution?
options: Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same probability
quizQuestions.answer: Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same probability
quizQuestions.explanation: BINS: Binary (two outcomes), Independent trials, Number of trials n fixed, Same probability p each trial.

quizQuestions.question: For X ~ B(n=10, p=0.3), what is E(X)?
quizQuestions.explanation: E(X) = n*p = 10 * 0.3 = 3. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Binomial & Geometric Distributions.

quizQuestions.question: For X ~ B(n=10, p=0.3), what is SD(X)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Var(X) = n*p*(1-p) = 10*0.3*0.7 = 2.1. SD(X) = sqrt(2.1). It connects the correct answer to Binomial & Geometric Distributions and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: In Binomial & Geometric Distributions, P(X = k) for a binomial is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Binomial probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k). Students need to recognize why C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k) matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement: The BINS conditions for a binomial distribution is...
options: Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same probability
quizQuestions.answer: Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same probability
quizQuestions.explanation: The best answer is Binary, Independent, Number fixed, Same probability. It directly fits the prompt, while the other choices reflect different course ideas, incomplete definitions, or common mistakes.

quizQuestions.question: The Central Limit Theorem states that for large n, the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: The CLT states that for large n, the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately Normal regardless of the population shape.

quizQuestions.question: The standard error of x-bar is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x-bar = sigma / sqrt(n).

quizQuestions.question: As sample size n increases, the standard error of x-bar:
quizQuestions.explanation: SE = sigma/sqrt(n). As n increases, SE decreases — larger samples give more precise estimates.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Central Limit Theorem, for large n the sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: For sufficiently large samples, the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal under the usual conditions.

quizQuestions.question: What is the standard error of x-bar when the population standard deviation is sigma and the sample size is n?
quizQuestions.explanation: The standard error of the sample mean is sigma divided by the square root of the sample size.

quizQuestions.question: A 95% confidence interval means:
options: 95% of the data falls in the interval
We are 95% sure the true parameter is in THIS interval
In repeated sampling, 95% of such intervals will contain the true parameter
The sample mean is within 5% of the true mean
quizQuestions.answer: In repeated sampling, 95% of such intervals will contain the true parameter
quizQuestions.explanation: A 95% CI means that if we repeated the procedure many times, 95% of the constructed intervals would contain the true parameter.

quizQuestions.question: The margin of error in a confidence interval equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: Margin of error = z* * SE (critical value times standard error). It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Confidence Intervals.

quizQuestions.question: To cut the margin of error in half, you must multiply the sample size by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Since ME = z*/sqrt(n), to halve ME you need sqrt(n) to double, so n must quadruple.

quizQuestions.question: What is the z* critical value for a 90% confidence interval?
quizQuestions.explanation: For 90% CI, the middle 90% leaves 5% in each tail. z* = 1.645. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Confidence Intervals, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: ___ is the z* critical value for a 90% confidence interval.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 1.645.

quizQuestions.question: The null hypothesis H0 typically states:
options: The sample mean equals the population mean
quizQuestions.explanation: H0 is the statement of no effect, no difference, or no change — the status quo.

quizQuestions.question: In Significance Tests, The p-value is:
options: The probability that H0 is true
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the data, assuming H0 is true
The probability that Ha is true
quizQuestions.answer: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the data, assuming H0 is true
quizQuestions.explanation: The p-value = P(data as extreme or more | H0 is true). Small p-value is evidence against H0.

quizQuestions.question: If p-value < alpha, you should:
quizQuestions.explanation: If p-value < alpha, the result is statistically significant: we reject H0 in favor of Ha.

quizQuestions.question: In Significance Tests, A Type I error is:
options: Failing to reject a false H0
Failing to reject a true H0
quizQuestions.explanation: Type I error (false positive): Rejecting H0 when it is actually true. Its probability equals alpha.

quizQuestions.question: In Significance Tests, A Type II error is:
options: Failing to reject a false H0
quizQuestions.answer: Failing to reject a false H0
quizQuestions.explanation: Type II error (false negative): Failing to reject H0 when H0 is actually false. Its probability is beta.

quizQuestions.question: What does the null hypothesis usually state in a significance test?
options: There is no effect or no difference
The alternative hypothesis is definitely true
The sample statistic equals the p-value
quizQuestions.answer: There is no effect or no difference
quizQuestions.explanation: The null hypothesis is the baseline claim, often stating no effect, no difference, or no association.

quizQuestions.question: For a two-sample t-test comparing means of two independent groups, H0 is typically:
quizQuestions.explanation: H0: mu1 - mu2 = 0 (equivalently mu1 = mu2) — no difference in population means.

quizQuestions.question: A paired t-test is used when:
quizQuestions.explanation: A paired t-test is appropriate when observations are naturally paired — such as before/after on the same individuals.

quizQuestions.question: When testing H0: p1=p2, the pooled estimate of p used is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The pooled proportion p-hat = (x1+x2)/(n1+n2) is used to calculate the test statistic.

quizQuestions.question: For a two-sample t-test comparing independent means, what is the usual null hypothesis?
quizQuestions.explanation: The usual null hypothesis for two independent means states that the population mean difference is zero.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A conservative approximation for two-sample t degrees of freedom is...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "min(n1-1, n2-1)" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test is used to:
options: Test if observed categorical data fits a hypothesized distribution
Test independence of two quantitative variables
quizQuestions.answer: Test if observed categorical data fits a hypothesized distribution
quizQuestions.explanation: The chi-square goodness-of-fit test determines whether the distribution of a single categorical variable matches a specified distribution.

quizQuestions.question: The chi-square test statistic is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Chi-square statistic = sum of [(O - E)^2 / E] for all categories. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Chi-Square Tests, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: For a valid chi-square test, expected counts should generally be:
quizQuestions.explanation: The condition for the chi-square test is that all expected counts are at least 5.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a chi-square goodness-of-fit test?
options: Test whether observed categorical counts fit a hypothesized distribution
Measure the strength of a linear relationship
quizQuestions.answer: Test whether observed categorical counts fit a hypothesized distribution
quizQuestions.explanation: A goodness-of-fit test compares observed counts across categories with expected counts from a stated distribution.

quizQuestions.question: In regression inference, H0 for the slope is:
quizQuestions.explanation: H0: beta = 0 tests whether there is a statistically significant linear relationship in the population.

quizQuestions.question: The conditions for regression inference are summarized as:
options: Linearity, Independence, Normality of residuals, Equal variance (LINE)
quizQuestions.answer: Linearity, Independence, Normality of residuals, Equal variance (LINE)
quizQuestions.explanation: Conditions: Linear relationship, Independent observations, Normal residuals, Equal/constant variance. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Inference for Linear Regression.

quizQuestions.question: The t-statistic for the slope in regression output is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The t-statistic for the slope = b1 / SE(b1). This reinforces the Inference for Linear Regression idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Degrees of freedom for the t-test for regression slope with n data points is:
quizQuestions.explanation: For simple linear regression (one predictor), df = n - 2. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Inference for Linear Regression.

quizQuestions.question: In regression inference, what is the usual null hypothesis for the population slope?
quizQuestions.explanation: The usual null hypothesis says the population slope is zero, meaning no linear relationship in the population.

quizQuestions.question: In a regression-inference case study, which condition checks whether a linear model is appropriate?
options: The scatterplot and residual plot show no strong curvature
The sample mean equals the sample median
The explanatory variable is measured in percentages
The response variable has exactly two categories
quizQuestions.answer: The scatterplot and residual plot show no strong curvature
quizQuestions.explanation: Regression inference for slope assumes the linear model is a reasonable description of the relationship.

quizQuestions.question: What does a confidence interval for the slope estimate?
options: The predicted y-value for every individual
A plausible range for the population regression slope
The correlation in the sample only
The intercept of the least-squares line
quizQuestions.answer: A plausible range for the population regression slope
quizQuestions.explanation: The interval estimates the population slope beta, not just the sample slope b.

quizQuestions.question: If a residual plot shows increasing spread as x increases, which condition is most directly threatened?
quizQuestions.explanation: Regression inference assumes roughly equal variability of residuals across x-values.

quizQuestions.question: For AP Statistics, the correct conclusion to a significance test should include:
options: A decision in context about the population parameter
A statement that the null hypothesis is proven true
quizQuestions.answer: A decision in context about the population parameter
quizQuestions.explanation: AP scoring expects students to connect the test result back to the parameter and real context.

quizQuestions.question: Which phrase correctly interprets a p-value?
options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability, assuming the null is true, of getting a result as extreme or more extreme than the observed result
The probability that the alternative is false
The probability that the sample was random
quizQuestions.answer: The probability, assuming the null is true, of getting a result as extreme or more extreme than the observed result
quizQuestions.explanation: A p-value is conditional on the null hypothesis and measures extremeness of the observed statistic.

quizQuestions.question: Which review task best prepares students for mixed AP Statistics free-response questions?
options: Practicing parameter identification, condition checks, calculation, and contextual conclusion together
Using the same test for every scenario
quizQuestions.answer: Practicing parameter identification, condition checks, calculation, and contextual conclusion together
quizQuestions.explanation: Free-response success depends on choosing the correct procedure and communicating assumptions, work, and conclusion.

questions.question: A researcher records the blood type (A, B, AB, O) of 50 patients. Blood type is best classified as:
questions.explanation: Blood type is categorical with no natural order among categories, making it nominal.

questions.question: For the dataset {2, 4, 4, 6, 8, 8, 8, 10}, what is the median?
questions.explanation: With 8 values, median = average of 4th and 5th values = (6+8)/2 = 7. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in AP Statistics midterm concepts.

questions.question: A z-score of -1.5 means the observation is:
questions.explanation: z = (x - mu)/sigma. z = -1.5 means the observation is 1.5 standard deviations below the mean.

questions.question: In a scatterplot, what does r = -0.95 indicate?
options: A nonlinear association with positive slope
questions.explanation: A correlation close to -1 indicates a strong negative linear association. It connects the correct answer to AP Statistics midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Which principle ensures causal conclusions in an experiment?
questions.explanation: Random assignment of subjects to treatments balances confounding variables, allowing causal inference.

questions.question: P(A) = 0.6, P(B) = 0.3, and A and B are independent. What is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B) = 0.6 * 0.3 = 0.18. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in AP Statistics midterm concepts.

questions.question: X can equal 1, 2, or 3 with probabilities 0.2, 0.5, 0.3. What is E(X)?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 2.1.

questions.question: Fifteen independent Bernoulli trials each have success probability 0.4. Which distribution models the number of successes?
questions.explanation: A fixed number of independent Bernoulli trials with the same success probability is modeled by a binomial distribution.

questions.question: Population mean=100, SD=20, n=25. What is the standard error of x-bar?
questions.explanation: SE = sigma/sqrt(n) = 20/sqrt(25) = 20/5 = 4. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in AP Statistics midterm concepts.

questions.question: A symmetric, bell-shaped distribution — which measure of center is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: For symmetric distributions, the mean is preferred as it uses all data values.

questions.question: 1,000 randomly selected adults are polled. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Randomly selecting individuals from the population without regard to subgroups describes an SRS.

questions.question: In a boxplot, the box spans which part of the data?
questions.explanation: The box in a boxplot extends from the first quartile to the third quartile.

questions.question: r = 0.87 between ice cream sales and drowning rates. Concluding ice cream causes drowning is an example of:
questions.explanation: Warm weather is a confounding variable causing both. Correlation does not imply causation.

questions.question: Multiplying all values in a dataset by 3 will:
options: Triple both the mean and standard deviation
questions.answer: Triple both the mean and standard deviation
questions.explanation: Multiplying by k multiplies all measures of center and spread by k. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in AP Statistics midterm concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: The LSRL is y-hat = -130 + 4.2x. Predict the weight of a person 65 inches tall.
questions.explanation: y-hat = -130 + 4.2(65) = -130 + 273 = 143 lbs. Students need to recognize why 143 lbs matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: A 95 percent confidence interval for a population mean test score is (72, 84). Which interpretation is correct?
options: 95 percent of students scored between 72 and 84
We are 95 percent confident the population mean score is between 72 and 84
There is a 95 percent chance the sample mean changes to 72
questions.answer: We are 95 percent confident the population mean score is between 72 and 84
questions.explanation: A confidence interval estimates a population parameter, not the percentage of individual observations in the interval.

questions.question: A significance test yields p-value = 0.03, alpha = 0.05. The correct conclusion is:
options: Fail to reject H0; no significant evidence
Reject H0; statistically significant evidence against H0
questions.answer: Reject H0; statistically significant evidence against H0
questions.explanation: p-value = 0.03 < alpha = 0.05, so we reject H0. It connects the correct answer to AP Statistics final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: For X ~ B(20, 0.5), P(X = 10) = ?
questions.explanation: P(X=10) = C(20,10) * (0.5)^10 * (0.5)^10 = C(20,10) * (0.5)^20. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from AP Statistics final concepts.

questions.question: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test with 5 categories: df = ?
questions.explanation: For a goodness-of-fit test with k categories, df = k - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4. Students need to recognize why 4 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: In a medical test for a disease, which situation is a Type I error?
options: Diagnosing a healthy person as sick
Diagnosing a sick person as healthy
questions.answer: Diagnosing a healthy person as sick
questions.explanation: A Type I error rejects a true null hypothesis; in this context, it is a false positive diagnosis.

questions.question: In regression output, slope b1 = 2.5 with SE(b1) = 0.8. The t-statistic for testing H0: beta=0 is:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 3.125.

questions.question: A 95% CI for p1 - p2 is (-0.05, 0.15). This means:
options: No significant difference between p1 and p2 at alpha=0.05
questions.answer: No significant difference between p1 and p2 at alpha=0.05
questions.explanation: 0 is in the interval (-0.05, 0.15), so we fail to find significant evidence of a difference.

questions.question: The power of a test is defined as:
options: The probability of a Type I error
questions.explanation: Power = 1 - beta = P(reject H0 | H0 is false) = probability of correctly detecting a real effect.

questions.question: If X has mean 10 and standard deviation 3, and Y = 2X - 1, what are the mean and standard deviation of Y?
questions.explanation: For Y = 2X - 1, the mean is 2(10) - 1 = 19 and the standard deviation is |2| times 3 = 6.

questions.question: To test if gender and product preference are independent, use:
questions.explanation: To test whether two categorical variables are independent within one population, use chi-square test of independence.

questions.question: Which increases the width of a confidence interval?
questions.explanation: A higher confidence level requires a larger critical value z*, which increases the margin of error and widens the interval.

questions.question: Sampling distribution of p-hat: n=100, p=0.6. The standard error is:
questions.explanation: SE = sqrt(p*(1-p)/n) = sqrt(0.6*0.4/100) = sqrt(0.0024) = 0.049. This reinforces the AP Statistics final concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: A paired t-test is conducted on which values?
options: The differences within each matched pair
The pooled count in each category
questions.answer: The differences within each matched pair
questions.explanation: A paired t-test analyzes the within-pair differences, such as after minus before for each subject.

questions.question: Which plot checks equal variance (homoscedasticity) in regression inference?
questions.explanation: A residual plot should show no pattern and constant spread — checking linearity and equal variance.

questions.question: Which method divides the population into groups by characteristic and randomly samples from each?
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling: divide into strata sharing a characteristic, then randomly sample from each stratum.

questions.question: Chi-square test of homogeneity comparing 3 populations with 4 categorical outcomes: df = ?
questions.explanation: df = (rows - 1)*(columns - 1) = (3-1)*(4-1) = 2*3 = 6. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in AP Statistics final concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: In a residual plot for regression inference, what pattern supports the linear model conditions?
options: Random scatter around zero with no clear pattern
questions.answer: Random scatter around zero with no clear pattern
questions.explanation: Residuals should be randomly scattered around zero with no systematic pattern and roughly constant spread.

questions.question: To estimate a proportion within 3% at 95% confidence with no prior estimate of p, sample size should be:
If the population is already normally distributed, the sampling distribution of x-bar is normal for:
questions.explanation: If the population is normal, then the sampling distribution of x-bar is exactly normal for ANY sample size n.

questions.question: Students who eat breakfast score higher. This is observational. Why can't we conclude causation?
options: The sample size is too small
Confounding variables (e.g., parental involvement) may explain both behaviors
Correlation is always zero in observational studies
questions.answer: Confounding variables (e.g., parental involvement) may explain both behaviors
questions.explanation: In observational studies, confounding variables may be responsible for the observed association — causation cannot be established.
